title: The Node.js Event Loop: libuv Phases, Timers & `nextTick`
description: Step through the **server-side** event loop, the one libuv runs — not the browser model. Walk the phase cycle (timers, pending callbacks, poll, check, close), then settle the classics: `setImmediate` vs `setTimeout(0)` vs `process.nextTick`, and exactly where microtasks drain between phases. Every ordering puzzle here was executed and verified on Node.js. Explanation-first feedback reveals the *why* after each answer.

questions.title: Which component actually owns and runs the event loop in Node.js?

options.text: **libuv**, the C library Node is built on

questions.explanation:
V8 only *executes JavaScript*: it runs your call stack and owns the **Promise microtask queue**. It has no event loop, no timers, and no sockets.

The loop itself — the phase machine, the thread pool for file I/O, and cross-platform async networking — all come from **libuv**, the C library Node embeds. That is why "the event loop" here is an operating-system concept, not a browser one, and why the phase model below looks nothing like the browser task/microtask picture.

Analogy: V8 is the engine that runs each chef’s recipe; **libuv is the kitchen expediter** deciding which ticket the kitchen works on next.
questions.references:
[libuv — Design overview of the event loop](https://docs.libuv.org/en/v1.x/design.html)

[Node.js — The event loop, timers, and `process.nextTick()`](https://nodejs.org/en/learn/asynchronous-work/event-loop-timers-and-nexttick)

questions.title: Each turn of the loop runs its **user-observable** phases in which order?
questions.explanation:
One loop turn always proceeds in this fixed order:

- **timers** — fires due `setTimeout` / `setInterval` callbacks.
- **pending callbacks** — runs a few system callbacks deferred from the last turn (e.g. some TCP errors like `ECONNREFUSED`).
- **poll** — retrieves new I/O events and runs most I/O callbacks; this is where the loop *blocks and waits* when idle.
- **check** — fires `setImmediate` callbacks, immediately after poll.
- **close callbacks** — runs `close` handlers (e.g. `socket.on('close', ...)`).

Node’s diagram also shows an internal **idle, prepare** box between pending callbacks and poll, but it is libuv-internal and you can never schedule into it — hence "user-observable" above. After the last phase the loop wraps back to timers and repeats.

questions.title: Which of these callbacks are executed during the **timers** phase?
questions.explanation:
The **timers** phase is dedicated to threshold timers: `setTimeout` and `setInterval`. It runs every timer whose delay has *elapsed* by the time the loop reaches this phase.

The two distractors belong elsewhere: `setImmediate` runs in the **check** phase, and an I/O completion callback such as `fs.readFile` runs in the **poll** phase. Keeping these straight is the whole game — most ordering surprises come from misremembering which scheduler maps to which phase.

questions.title: What makes the **poll** phase different from every other phase?

options.text: It runs ready I/O callbacks **and can block, waiting** for new I/O when nothing else is scheduled
It is the phase where Promises resolve

questions.explanation:
Poll is where a Node process spends its *idle* time. It has two jobs: run the I/O callbacks whose events are ready, and calculate **how long to block** waiting for the next I/O event.

That blocking is the clever part. If `setImmediate` callbacks are queued, poll refuses to block — it ends early and jumps to **check**. If a timer is about to come due, it wraps back to **timers**. Otherwise it parks on the OS (epoll/kqueue/IOCP) until a socket or file is ready.

This is precisely how an idle web server "waits" for the next request without burning a single CPU cycle.

questions.title: `setImmediate(fn)` callbacks execute in which event-loop phase? (one word)
questions.explanation:
The **check** phase exists for one reason: to run `setImmediate` callbacks, right after the poll phase finishes.

Mentally pin it next to poll. "I just finished handling I/O (poll) — now run my `setImmediate` work (check)." That poll → check adjacency is what makes `setImmediate` so predictable inside I/O callbacks, as later questions show.
questions.references:
[Node.js API — `setImmediate()`](https://nodejs.org/api/timers.html#setimmediatecallback-args)

[Node.js — The event loop, timers, and `process.nextTick()`](https://nodejs.org/en/learn/asynchronous-work/event-loop-timers-and-nexttick)

questions.title: A TCP socket emits its `'close'` event. Which phase fires that handler?
questions.explanation:
When a handle such as a socket or server shuts down, its `'close'` callback is deferred to the dedicated **close callbacks** phase at the tail of the loop turn — `socket.on('close', ...)` lands here, and an abrupt `socket.destroy()` routes its close callback here too.

Think of it as the loop’s **cleanup desk**: tear-down work happens after the real I/O and timers of the turn are done, just before the loop wraps around again.

questions.title: Where does `process.nextTick` sit in the libuv phase model?

options.text: Nowhere — it is **not a libuv phase**; its queue drains right after the current operation, *between* phases, before the loop advances
It is the very first phase, running before `timers`
It runs only inside the `poll` phase
It is just another name for the `check` phase

questions.explanation:
`process.nextTick` is a **Node-level queue**, not a libuv phase. The moment any chunk of JavaScript finishes — the initial script, or any single phase callback — Node empties the *entire* nextTick queue before doing anything else (and before the Promise microtask queue).

So despite the name, it is not "next loop iteration." It is "**right now**, before the loop is allowed to move on." `setImmediate`, confusingly, is the one that actually fires on a later iteration. The names are historically backwards; the behavior is what matters.

questions.title: In what order do these four lines log?
questions.explanation:
Synchronous code wins outright: the call stack must empty before *any* queue is touched, so `A` and `D` log first, in source order.

Then the microtask checkpoint runs, and it has a strict priority: the **nextTick queue drains before the Promise microtask queue**. So `B` (nextTick) beats `C` (promise). Only after both are empty would the loop reach its first real phase.

Result: `A, D, B, C`. The trap is assuming `nextTick`/`then` interleave with the synchronous logs — they cannot; nothing async runs until the stack is clear.

questions.title: Between two phases, Node runs a "microtask checkpoint." Which callbacks drain there?
Inside a file-read callback, when does the code *after* `await` run?
questions.explanation:
`await null` suspends the async function and schedules its continuation as a **Promise microtask**. The synchronous part of the callback finishes first — but all it did was queue `immediate` into the **check** phase.

We are still in the **poll** phase. Before the loop can leave poll, it runs the microtask checkpoint: the continuation resumes (`after-await`), which queues a `nextTick`; the checkpoint keeps draining and runs `nextTick`. *Only now* does the loop advance to **check** and log `immediate`.

The trap: `await` does **not** hand control to the next phase. It resumes inside the *same* phase’s microtask drain — so both `after-await` and the `nextTick` it spawns beat the already-queued `setImmediate`.

questions.title: Why can a recursive `process.nextTick` freeze an entire Node server?

options.text: The nextTick queue must be **fully emptied** before the loop can advance, so it never reaches poll — I/O and timers starve
It overflows the call stack and throws a `RangeError`
Each `nextTick` waits ~1 ms, so the server only gets a little slower
It saturates the 4 libuv thread-pool threads

questions.explanation:
The checkpoint promises to **empty** the nextTick queue before the loop continues. If every callback enqueues another, the queue is never empty, so the loop is pinned *between* phases forever: no **poll** (no new connections accepted), no **timers**, nothing.

It does *not* overflow the stack — each `spin` returns cleanly before the next is invoked. And it is pure JavaScript, so the libuv thread pool (used for file I/O, DNS, crypto) is irrelevant here.

The fix when you want to yield is `setImmediate(spin)`: it reschedules onto a *future* loop turn, letting I/O and timers run in between.

questions.title: At the **top level** of a module, what is the output order here?

options.text: The order is **not guaranteed** — it can differ from run to run and between machines
They fire simultaneously, so the order is random *within the same tick*

questions.explanation:
This is the famous coin-flip. At startup the result depends on how much wall-clock time passes before the loop first reaches the **timers** phase. A `setTimeout(…, 0)` is clamped to **1 ms**, so:

- if the loop reaches timers ≥ 1 ms after start, the timer is due → `timeout` wins;
- if it gets there faster, the timer is not due yet → `immediate` (check) wins on the next pass.

That margin hinges on process startup cost and machine load, so the spec guarantees nothing. On a fast, idle box you may see one order *consistently* — but it is not contractual, so never rely on it. The ambiguity disappears the instant you move both calls **inside an I/O callback** (next question).

questions.title: Move the very same two calls **inside an I/O callback**. Now what?

options.text: Still non-deterministic, just like the top-level version
Only `immediate` runs; the timer is silently dropped

questions.explanation:
The `readFile` callback runs in the **poll** phase. The *very next* phase in the same turn is **check**, so the freshly-scheduled `setImmediate` fires before the loop can possibly wrap around to the **timers** phase on the following turn.

Poll → check adjacency makes this rock-solid: **inside any I/O callback, `setImmediate` beats `setTimeout(…, 0)` 100% of the time.** This is the single best mnemonic for remembering the phase order — if you can recall this example, you can reconstruct the whole cycle.

questions.title: You schedule `setTimeout(fn, 10)`, then run a 60 ms synchronous loop. When does `fn` fire?

options.text: After **~60 ms** — the delay is a *minimum*, and the timers phase cannot run until the stack is clear
After exactly 10 ms, on a background thread
Never — it already expired during the loop, so it is discarded

questions.explanation:
A timer delay is a **floor, not a deadline**. `setTimeout` only promises the callback will not run *earlier* than the delay — there is no upper bound.

While your `while` loop hogs the single main thread, the event loop cannot even reach the **timers** phase, so the due timer simply waits. Running this prints something like `fired after ~60ms` — not 10, and not 70.

This is why one slow synchronous handler delays *every* pending timer and *every* queued I/O callback at once, and why "don’t block the event loop" is rule #1 for Node servers.

questions.title: Node clamps any `setTimeout` delay below 1 up to a floor. So `setTimeout(fn, 0)` is treated as how many milliseconds? (number only)
questions.explanation:
Per the `setTimeout` docs, when `delay` is less than 1 (or larger than the 32-bit signed max, ~2.1 billion), Node sets it to **1 ms**. So `setTimeout(fn, 0)` and `setTimeout(fn, 1)` request the exact same minimum delay.

It is a tiny detail with real consequences: it is part of *why* a top-level `setTimeout(…, 0)` can lose the startup race to `setImmediate` — the timer is not actually due for ~1 ms, giving the **check** phase a chance to win first.

questions.title: A server warms up: it reads a config file, then schedules four things. What order logs?
questions.explanation:
Every rule from this quiz lands in one trace. Stepping through:

1. The callback body runs synchronously top-to-bottom: `1`, then `6`. Everything else was only *scheduled*.
2. The callback returns → **microtask checkpoint**: nextTick first (`4`), then Promise (`5`).
3. We are in **poll**; the adjacent next phase is **check** → `3: immediate`.
4. The loop wraps to the next turn’s **timers** phase → `2: timeout`.

Final order: **`1, 6, 4, 5, 3, 2`** — sync → nextTick → promise → check → timers. Note `immediate` (`3`) beats `timeout` (`2`) because we started inside an I/O callback.

questions.title: This snippet proves microtasks drain between *every* phase. What is the order?
questions.explanation:
Walk the phases:

1. In **poll**, the callback queues a nextTick, a `setImmediate`, and a `setTimeout`, then returns → checkpoint drains `A`.
2. **check** runs `B`, which itself queues another nextTick and a promise.
3. Leaving check — *before* the loop reaches the next **timers** phase — the checkpoint drains again: `C` (nextTick) then `D` (promise).
4. The next turn’s **timers** phase finally runs `E`.

Order: **`A, B, C, D, E`**. The lesson: the microtask checkpoint is **not** once-per-loop — it fires after *each* phase. That is exactly why `C` and `D` sneak in ahead of `E`.
questions.references:
[Node.js — The event loop, timers, and `process.nextTick()`](https://nodejs.org/en/learn/asynchronous-work/event-loop-timers-and-nexttick)

[Node.js API — `process.nextTick()`](https://nodejs.org/api/process.html#processnexttickcallback-args)

questions.title: You must process a huge job in chunks without freezing incoming requests. Which call yields control between chunks?

options.text: `setImmediate(nextChunk)` — it reschedules onto a future **check** phase, so the loop still visits poll and timers in between
`process.nextTick(nextChunk)` — it fully yields before each chunk
Neither — only a Worker thread can ever avoid blocking
